card.title: Why Eyewitnesses Are So Often Wrong
card.teaser: You saw his face. You're sure. Juries believe you. The problem? Memory is not a recording — it's a remix.
card.coverImagePrompt: A blurred memory-like scene of a courtroom witness stand, a single spotlight on a pointing finger, faces around it dissolving into smoke, painterly surreal

lessons.title: Eleven years for the wrong man
lessons.body: In 1984, Jennifer Thompson was raped in her North Carolina apartment. She memorized her attacker's face with laser focus, confident she could identify him anywhere. She picked Ronald Cotton out of a lineup. Cotton spent eleven years in prison before DNA evidence cleared him — and identified the real rapist, a man whose face Jennifer had never even seen.

visual.imagePrompt: A 1980s police photo lineup card with six grainy black-and-white headshots, one marked with a shaky hand-drawn circle, dramatic shadows

lessons.title: Memory is reconstructive
lessons.body: We experience memory like playing a video — but that's an illusion. The brain stores fragments — a voice, a color, an emotion — and rebuilds the scene every time we remember it, filling gaps with what seems plausible. Every retrieval is an edit. The version you recall at trial two years later is not the version you stored at the scene.

keyTerms.definition: The idea that remembering is an act of rebuilding, not playback
How post-event information (questions, news, conversations) alters what witnesses 'remember'

visual.imagePrompt: An illustration of a face made of scattered jigsaw pieces, some missing, others being placed by a ghostly hand, dreamlike coloring

lessons.title: Elizabeth Loftus and the smashed cars
lessons.body: In a landmark 1974 experiment, psychologist Elizabeth Loftus showed volunteers a video of a car crash, then asked half of them how fast the cars were going when they 'smashed' into each other. The other half got the word 'contacted'. The 'smashed' group not only estimated higher speeds — a week later, many of them falsely remembered seeing broken glass that wasn't in the video.

visual.imagePrompt: A vintage black-and-white photograph of a staged two-car collision, with subtle visual glitches suggesting altered perception, lab monitor framing

lessons.title: How lineups go wrong
lessons.body: Of the first 375 DNA exonerations in the US, roughly 70% involved a mistaken eyewitness ID. The usual culprits: lineups where the suspect stands out, officers who unconsciously signal which face is the 'right' one, and witnesses who grow more confident every time they see the same face again. By trial, that early uncertainty has hardened into certainty — and juries love certainty.
lessons.funFact: Cross-race identifications are especially unreliable: people recognize faces of their own race roughly 1.4x more accurately than faces of other races.

visual.imagePrompt: A modern police lineup behind one-way glass, the suspect subtly spotlit brighter than the others, tense interrogation atmosphere

lessons.title: What better looks like
lessons.body: Psychologists now push for 'double-blind sequential lineups' — showing faces one at a time, with an officer who doesn't know who the suspect is, and recording the witness's confidence immediately. Jurisdictions that adopted these reforms have seen fewer mistaken IDs. Your memory isn't broken — it just works nothing like the HD footage our justice system tends to assume.

quiz.question: Roughly what share of early US DNA exonerations involved a mistaken eyewitness?
quiz.explanation: Eyewitness misidentification was a factor in approximately 70% of the first 375 DNA exonerations.

quiz.question: Elizabeth Loftus's car-crash study mainly showed that…
options: Wording of questions can plant false memories
Speed is impossible to estimate accurately
quiz.explanation: Changing one verb altered both speed estimates and, later, whether people remembered seeing broken glass that wasn't there.

quiz.question: Which reform helps reduce lineup errors?
options: Letting officers tell witnesses if they picked the right person
Showing all faces simultaneously in a group
Double-blind sequential lineups with recorded confidence
Using only photos of similar-aged suspects
quiz.explanation: Double-blind sequential lineups remove experimenter bias and capture a witness's initial confidence, which tends to inflate over time.

sources.title: Reconstruction of automobile destruction: An example of the interaction between language and memory
sources.publisher: Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior

sources.title: Identifying the Culprit: Assessing Eyewitness Identification
